worth_it_explanation: Pacing decides whether a flight spreads its impression or revenue goal evenly across its run or burns through it early, so it sets whether an advertiser deal lands on target or finishes short. A flight that paces by default, or one whose under-delivery is never watched until the end date passes, can miss a goal that a mid-flight adjustment would have saved. The check is a review of pacing settings and delivery reporting an operator already has in Kevel, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete delivery decision the operator can act on while the flight is still live.
intro: Pacing controls how a flight delivers toward its goal over time, spreading impressions or revenue across the run rather than front-loading them or trailing off short. In Kevel, the Ad Decision Engine balances a flight toward its goal each day, but that balancing only works as intended when the flight carries a deliberate goal and pacing setting rather than defaults nobody chose. These checks confirm that flights with impression or revenue goals pace on purpose, and that a flight at risk of missing its goal is caught early through delivery reporting rather than discovered after the end date.

audit_checks.title: Flights with impression or revenue goals do not pace deliberately
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a flight carries an impression or revenue goal but its pacing is left to whatever the flight was created with, delivery can front-load early in the run or trail off short of the goal. Either pattern breaks the even delivery an advertiser deal usually assumes, so the flight can overspend its inventory in the first few days or finish under its committed numbers.

educate.base:
In Kevel, a flight is the level where delivery rules live, including its goal and how it paces toward that goal. A flight can carry a goal measured in impressions, clicks, conversions, a percentage, lifetime revenue, or daily revenue, and the Ad Decision Engine balances delivery so the flight reaches that goal by its end date. The balancer recalculates each day, so if a slow weekend leaves a flight behind, it aims to deliver more in the days that follow to stay on track. Kevel describes the goal of pacing as showing the ad evenly across the run rather than as fast as possible, for example spreading a 30M-impression deal evenly across the 30 days of June rather than clearing it in the first week.

The risk is not the balancer. The balancer does its job once a flight has a goal and an even-pacing intent. The risk is a flight that carries a goal in name but never had a deliberate pacing decision behind it, so it delivers in whatever shape the defaults and the available traffic produce. A flight set to deliver as fast as possible against an impression goal will front-load, clearing the goal early and leaving the rest of the run dark. A flight whose goal and dates do not match the traffic the inventory can supply will trail off short. In both cases the operator meant for the flight to pace and it did not.

An even-pacing intent matters most where the goal reflects a commitment. A direct-sold impression deal, a sponsorship that promised steady presence across a campaign window, or a daily revenue target that resets at midnight each all depend on the flight spreading delivery rather than spending it in a burst. Kevel separates pacing from capping for this reason. Capping stops serving once a hard threshold is reached, while pacing applies the balancing logic that smooths delivery across time, so a daily cap alone does not give a flight even pacing.

A few signs point to flights that carry goals without pacing deliberately:
  • A flight has an impression or revenue goal but is set to deliver as fast as possible rather than even, so it front-loads against the goal
  • A flight's goal amount and its start and end dates do not match the traffic the targeted inventory can realistically supply
  • Daily delivery for a flight spikes early in its run and falls off, rather than holding a steady shape across the dates
You may want to confirm that every flight with an impression or revenue goal carries a pacing intent that matches the commitment behind it. One approach is to list the active goal-bearing flights, check each flight's goal type and pacing setting, and read its daily delivery shape against an even line across its dates.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site or app running a Kevel-powered ad server, a direct-sold sponsorship or a guaranteed impression deal usually promises steady presence across the campaign window rather than a burst at the start. You may want to confirm those flights pace evenly so the advertiser sees consistent delivery across the run, rather than a flight that clears its impression goal in the first few days and goes dark.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a retail media setup where a retailer runs sponsored-product ads on its own site or app through Kevel, a brand often buys a flight tied to a sales window or a daily budget. You may want to confirm those flights pace toward an impression or daily revenue goal evenly across the window, so a brand's spend spreads across the period it paid for rather than exhausting early and leaving the later days without that sponsored placement.

investigate.intro: Review the active flights that carry impression or revenue goals, confirm each one's goal type and pacing setting, and read its daily delivery shape to see whether it paces evenly or front-loads.

kevel_ui.label: Kevel UI
steps: Open a campaign and its flights, and list the flights that carry a goal in impressions, lifetime revenue, or daily revenue.
For each goal-bearing flight, open it and note its goal type, goal amount, start and end dates, and whether it is set to pace evenly or to deliver as fast as possible.
Note any flight whose goal amount and date range look mismatched to the traffic its targeting can supply, which puts even pacing out of reach.

kevel_management_api.label: Kevel Management API
steps: GET the flights under each active campaign and record the GoalType, Impressions or goal amount, StartDate, EndDate, and the pacing or even-delivery setting on each.
Filter the list to flights with an impression or revenue goal, and confirm each carries an even-pacing intent rather than the as-fast-as-possible default.
Flag any goal-bearing flight whose pacing setting does not match the commitment the goal represents.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per goal-bearing flight, the goal type, the goal amount, the date range, and the pacing setting, then reads the daily delivery shape against an even line across the dates. A flight that carries an impression or revenue goal and paces evenly, with a goal and date range that fit the available traffic, passes. A flight with a goal that delivers as fast as possible, or whose goal and dates make even pacing impossible, is a fail recorded with the flight and the mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the goal type, goal amount, date range, and pacing setting for each goal-bearing flight above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs flights with impression or revenue goals that do not pace deliberately, so delivery front-loads early or trails off short of the goal rather than spreading evenly across the run. Kevel's Ad Decision Engine balances a flight toward its goal each day when the flight carries an even-pacing intent, but a goal-bearing flight set to deliver as fast as possible, or one whose goal and dates do not fit the available traffic, breaks that even delivery. {{voice_recommend}} listing the active goal-bearing flights, confirming each one's goal type and pacing setting, and reading its daily delivery shape against an even line across its dates so the pacing matches the commitment behind the goal. After the review, set even pacing on the flights that promised steady delivery and align any mismatched goal and date range to the traffic the inventory can supply.

audit_checks.title: Under-delivery risk is not monitored while flights are live
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A flight that is pacing behind its goal can still recover if the gap is caught while the flight is live, through a date extension, a targeting change, or a priority adjustment. When delivery is only checked after the end date, a flight that missed its goal is discovered too late to fix, which can mean make-goods, refunds, or a broken commitment to the advertiser.

educate.base:
Kevel's pacing aims to deliver close to 100% of a flight's goal across its run, with the actual result varying by a few percent because request traffic varies day to day. That variation is normal. The problem is a flight whose pace is drifting toward a real miss, where the traffic the targeting can reach is not enough to hit the goal by the end date, and nobody sees it until the flight is already over.

The balancer reacts to delivery within the bounds of the available traffic. It can shift more weight to a flight that fell behind on a slow day, but it cannot create demand or impressions that the targeted inventory does not produce. So a flight with a goal set too high for its inventory, or a targeting filter narrower than the goal assumes, will keep pacing behind no matter how the balancer reweights it. Catching that gap depends on watching delivery against goal while the flight is live, not on the engine alone.

Kevel surfaces delivery data that supports this watching. The Real Time Reporting API updates roughly every five minutes and gives a near-immediate read on impressions, clicks, conversions, and revenue, while the Reporting API builds custom and scheduled reports grouped by dimensions such as flight. Together they let an operator compare what a flight has delivered against where an even pace would put it on a given day, so a flight falling behind shows up as a widening gap rather than a surprise at the end. A flight caught mid-run can still be helped, for example by extending the end date, widening targeting, or raising priority, while a flight reviewed only after it ends offers no such room.

A few signs point to under-delivery that is not being monitored:
  • No report or alert compares a flight's delivered impressions or revenue against its expected pace partway through the run
  • Flights that missed their goal are discovered after the end date rather than flagged while they were still live
  • The operator cannot say, for a flight currently running, whether it is ahead of, on, or behind its goal pace today
You may want to confirm that goal-bearing flights are watched against their expected pace while they run. One approach is to schedule a recurring report grouped by flight that shows delivered versus goal, and to set a threshold that flags any live flight pacing far enough behind to need a mid-run fix.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site or app running a Kevel-powered ad server, a guaranteed impression deal that misses its goal usually means a make-good or a refund to the advertiser. You may want to watch those flights against their expected pace during the run, so a flight falling behind can be extended or have its targeting widened before the end date passes rather than settled with a make-good after the fact.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a retail media setup where a retailer runs sponsored-product ads on its own site or app through Kevel, a brand's flight tied to a sales window has no value if it under-delivers after the window closes. You may want to monitor delivery against goal mid-flight, so a sponsored-product flight pacing behind during the sales period can be adjusted while the period is still open and the spend can still land.

investigate.intro: Review whether any reporting compares a live flight's delivery against its expected pace partway through the run, and whether a process exists to flag a flight pacing behind in time to act on it.

kevel_ui.label: Kevel UI
steps: Open the reporting interface and check whether a custom or scheduled report grouped by flight shows delivered impressions or revenue against each flight's goal.
For a sample of live goal-bearing flights, compare delivered to date against where an even pace would put the flight today, and note any that are running behind.
Ask the operator how a flight pacing behind gets noticed and who acts on it, and record whether that happens during the run or only after the end date.

kevel_management_api.label: Kevel Management API
steps: GET the active flights and record each one's goal amount, start date, and end date so the expected pace for today can be computed.
For each live flight, work out where an even pace would put delivery as of today against the total goal, to set the baseline to compare actual delivery against.

kevel_reporting_api.label: Kevel Reporting API
steps: Build a report grouped by flight with delivered impressions and revenue, using the Reporting API for custom and scheduled reports or the Real Time Reporting API for a near-immediate read.
Compare each live flight's delivered figure against its expected pace for the day, and record any flight far enough behind to risk missing its goal.
Confirm whether such a report runs on a recurring schedule so under-delivery is flagged while flights are live rather than checked by hand after the fact.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a recurring report or alert compares live flights' delivery against their expected pace, and whether a process exists to act on a flight flagged as pacing behind. A setup where goal-bearing flights are watched against their expected pace during the run, with a threshold that flags a behind flight in time to adjust it, passes. A setup where delivery is only reviewed after the end date, with no mid-run check on under-delivery, is a fail recorded with the flights left unmonitored.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a mid-run report compares live flights against their expected pace, and how a behind flight gets flagged, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor under-delivery while flights are live, so a flight pacing behind its goal is discovered after the end date rather than caught in time to fix. Kevel's balancer reweights a flight that falls behind, but it cannot create traffic the targeted inventory does not produce, so a flight with a goal set too high for its inventory keeps pacing behind unless someone catches the gap mid-run. {{voice_recommend}} scheduling a recurring report grouped by flight that shows delivered against goal, using the Reporting API or the near-real-time Real Time Reporting API, and setting a threshold that flags any live flight pacing far enough behind to need a date extension, a targeting change, or a priority adjustment. After that is in place, a flight at risk of missing its goal surfaces while it can still be helped rather than after the run has closed.
